title: Chiral Long-Range Order in three Euclidean Lattice Gross-Neveu Models
abstract: We prove the existence of Long-Range Order in a class of two-dimensional Euclidean lattice Gross-Neveu models with an even number of fermion flavors, covering three standard lattice discretizations, including naive and staggered fermions widely used in numerical studies. By performing a Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation, we map the fermionic systems to bosonic ones and establish Reflection Positivity for the resulting measures. Exploiting this structure, we combine Chessboard Estimates with a Peierls-type contour argument to prove Long-Range Order for the chirally charged fermion-mass bilinear $\overlineψψ$ at sufficiently small coupling and sufficiently large flavor number. Our analysis is robust with respect to the choice of lattice discretization and applies uniformly across different realizations of the same underlying continuum model. Moreover, we obtain uniform pointwise bounds on the bosonic two-point function, equivalently on the fermionic mass-mass correlator, showing that it is quantitatively controlled by the minimizers of the effective potential. This provides a fully rigorous and non-perturbative demonstration of Long-Range Order in lattice Gross-Neveu models and establishes a direct connection between the rigorous theory and its large-$N$ (mean-field) predictions.
summary: This paper rigorously proves the existence of Long-Range Order in various lattice formulations of the 2D Gross-Neveu model. It achieves this by transforming the fermionic problem into a bosonic one and applying sophisticated mathematical tools like Reflection Positivity and Peierls arguments. The result provides a non-perturbative validation of chiral symmetry breaking, connecting rigorous lattice theory to mean-field predictions.
main_problem: The central problem is rigorously proving the existence of Long-Range Order (LRO) in lattice regularizations of two-dimensional Euclidean Gross-Neveu models.
main_result: The authors provide a fully rigorous, non-perturbative demonstration of LRO for the chiral fermion bilinear $\langle\psi\psiangle$, establishing a direct link between the continuum theory and its large-$N$ mean-field predictions.
method: The analysis employs advanced techniques including Hubbard-Stratonovich transformations to map fermionic systems to bosonic ones, followed by using Reflection Positivity, Chessboard Estimates, and Peierls-type contour arguments.
model_or_system: The study focuses on three lattice discretizations of the 2D Euclidean Gross-Neveu model: Naive (NN), Staggered (SN), and Staggered Plaquette (SP). These models are used to investigate chiral symmetry breaking and dynamical mass generation.
key_observables: The primary observables include the chirally charged fermion-mass bilinear $\langle\psi\psiangle$ (the order parameter) and the bosonic two-point function, which controls the LRO.
important_parameters: LRO is proven for sufficiently small coupling constants ($\lambda$) and sufficiently large flavor numbers ($N$). The analysis compares results across different lattice discretizations.
main_assumptions: The proof relies on establishing Reflection Positivity for the resulting bosonic measures, which requires an even number of fermion flavors. The final characterization of infinite-volume states remains a future goal.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate phase distributions of anti-symmetrized forward Dirac operators and graphical representations related to Z2-Gauge Transformations used in the contour arguments.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes three lattice models (NN, SN, SP), establishing key properties of their respective Dirac operators. It then uses the Hubbard-Stratonovich transformation to derive an effective bosonic potential, proving Reflection Positivity and finally applying Peierls/Chessboard estimates to establish LRO.
why_interesting: While highly technical, the rigorous proof of spontaneous symmetry breaking (LRO) in a lattice field theory like GN is foundational for understanding how quantum fluctuations drive macroscopic order parameters, which has parallels in many-body physics relevant to AMO systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
